verses.text: The men of Ephraim gathered themselves together, and went northward, and said unto Jephthah, Wherefore passedst thou over to fight against the children of Ammon, and didst not call us to go with thee? we will burn thine house upon thee with fire. Jephthah said unto them, I and my people were at great strife with the children of Ammon; and when I called you, ye delivered me not out of their hands. When I saw that ye delivered me not, I put my life in my hands, and passed over against the children of Ammon, and the LORD delivered them into my hand: wherefore then are ye come up unto me this day, to fight against me? Guidance: Ephraim plays the same wounded-pride card it played with Gideon (8:1) — except now they upgrade the threat: 'we will burn your house upon you with fire.' Jephthah's reply is sharper than Gideon's diplomatic answer. He has no patience for it. He counters with two facts: I called you, you did not come; and the LORD gave the victory, not you. Honest rebuke replaces diplomacy. Both responses are biblical; sometimes peace requires soft words (Gideon), sometimes truth requires hard ones (Jephthah). Read the room you are in.

Then Jephthah gathered together all the men of Gilead, and fought with Ephraim: and the men of Gilead smote Ephraim, because they said, Ye Gileadites are fugitives of Ephraim among the Ephraimites, and among the Manassites. Guidance: Words start the war. Ephraim mocks the Gileadites as 'fugitives' — refugees, half-breeds, second-class Israelites. The insult lands in old wounds (Jephthah himself was thrust out of his father's house, v11:2). Civil war is rarely about land; it is usually about contempt. Identity-insults that an empire would shrug off can split a tribal federation in a generation.

The Gileadites took the passages of Jordan before the Ephraimites: and it was so, that when those Ephraimites which were escaped said, Let me go over; that the men of Gilead said unto him, Art thou an Ephraimite? If he said, Nay; then said they unto him, Say now Shibboleth: and he said Sibboleth: for he could not frame to pronounce it right. Then they took him, and slew him at the passages of Jordan: and there fell at that time of the Ephraimites forty and two thousand. Guidance: A regional pronunciation difference (Ephraim couldn't make the 'sh' sound; they said 'sibboleth' for 'shibboleth') becomes a death-test at the river crossings. The word 'shibboleth' itself means 'ear of grain' or 'flowing stream' — chosen because it required the consonant they couldn't produce. Forty-two thousand Ephraimites die because of a syllable. The chapter's quietest horror: civil war reduced to accent. Word 'shibboleth' has entered every language since to mean a tribal litmus test. The book of Judges has reached the place where tribes kill brothers over how they pronounce a vowel — and it gets worse in the chapters ahead.

Jephthah judged Israel six years. Then died Jephthah the Gileadite, and was buried in one of the cities of Gilead. Guidance: Six years — the shortest tenure of any major judge. The narrator does not name his burial city, only 'one of the cities of Gilead.' His life ends without a monument. The outcast warlord who saved Israel and lost his daughter is buried under a footnote. Even the Spirit-filled judges in Judges do not all get a full chapter on their funerals. Faithfulness is sometimes recorded in a single sentence and forgotten by the next paragraph.

After him Ibzan of Beth-lehem judged Israel. He had thirty sons, and thirty daughters, whom he sent abroad, and took in thirty daughters from abroad for his sons. He judged Israel seven years. Then died Ibzan, and was buried at Beth-lehem. Guidance: Ibzan's Bethlehem is probably the smaller Zebulun Bethlehem (Josh 19:15), not the Judean one. Thirty marriages out, thirty marriages in — sixty diplomatic alliances by family. The verb 'sent abroad' suggests his daughters married outside the clan and his sons brought in foreign wives. Whether they were Israelites of other tribes or actual foreigners the text leaves ambiguous. Seven years of peace bought partly by intermarriage politics. The book's recurring concern about intermarriage with non-Israelites means we should read Ibzan's biography with a slight tilt of the head.

After him Elon, a Zebulonite, judged Israel; and he judged Israel ten years. And Elon the Zebulonite died, and was buried in Aijalon in the country of Zebulun. Guidance: Elon ('oak') of Zebulun judged ten years and is buried in Aijalon ('field of deer'). Two verses, two place-names that share a root. The Spirit allots him a single paragraph. We are told nothing about his deeds, only that he held the role for a decade. The book is slowing down between the larger Jephthah-Samson stories — these minor judges are the connective tissue that the narrative is honest enough to record but not large enough to dwell on. Most servants of the LORD get this kind of biography: name, place, length, end. Faithfulness in obscurity is faithfulness all the same.

After him Abdon the son of Hillel, a Pirathonite, judged Israel. He had forty sons and thirty nephews, that rode on threescore and ten ass colts: and he judged Israel eight years. And Abdon the son of Hillel the Pirathonite died, and was buried in Pirathon in the land of Ephraim, in the mount of the Amalekites. Guidance: Forty sons, thirty nephews, seventy donkey colts — a clan-show of wealth and prestige (echoes of Jair, 10:4). Abdon ('servant') lives in Pirathon, in Ephraim — in territory once called 'the mount of the Amalekites,' a name that still hung on though Amalek had long been cleared from it. The verse plants a quiet detail that historians will use: Israel was at peace in places they once feared. Eight years, then the cycle resumes. The next chapter opens the Samson saga — and back to oppression.
